ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese eel porridge; 'porridge food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (rice-based porridge/congee) is the closest defensible code; no specific Vietnamese eel porridge code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese eel porridge; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Vietnamese eel porridge weighs approximately 400 g, providing around 450 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eel, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175136) combined with rice porridge/congee composition (FDC ID 169702), adapted for Vietnamese preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese eel porridge as a composite dish. Values are derived by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw freshwater eel (FDC ID 175136) and plain rice congee/porridge (FDC ID 169702), weighted to reflect a typical Vietnamese preparation ratio of approximately 30% eel meat and 70% rice porridge base, with adjustments for fish sauce sodium contribution. The input record's per-100g values (energy 112.5 kcal, protein 6.25 g, fat 3.75 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g) are consistent with this blended composition and are retained as the working estimate. FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted as a secondary cross-check for Southeast Asian eel and rice porridge values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice congee/porridge analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese eel porridge. GI estimated at 72 based on rice congee/porridge (GI ~72, high category) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice-based congee. The eel protein component (~6.25 g/100g) may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response in practice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (72 × 50 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 36.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from fish sauce and added salt; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and salt during preparation, targeting sodium below 200 mg per 100 g, and limit portion size to 200–250 g to keep single-meal sodium intake below 500 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice porridge base has a high glycemic index (~72) and a glycemic load of 36 per standard 400 g bowl, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation despite the diluting effect of high water content.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, avoid sweet condiments, pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycemic response, and monitor postprandial glucose when consuming rice-based porridges.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Eel provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that support cardiovascular health; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g per 100 g; however, sodium at 300 mg per 100 g requires monitoring.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may consume eel porridge in moderate portions (200–300 g) as the omega-3 content from eel is cardioprotective, but should request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium and avoid fried shallot garnishes to minimise oxidised fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eel omega-3 fatty acids and ginger gingerols provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from regular moderate consumption of eel porridge due to its omega-3 and anti-inflammatory herb content, while requesting fresh rather than fried shallots to avoid advanced glycation end-product contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cháo lươn (Vietnamese eel porridge) is a traditional Vietnamese comfort dish consisting of freshwater eel (lươn) simmered with white rice until the grains break down into a smooth, creamy porridge. The eel is typically cleaned, marinated with fish sauce, ginger, garlic, and shallots, then stir-fried briefly before being added to the simmering rice base. The dish is finished with fried shallots, spring onion, coriander, and sometimes fresh chilli. The texture is silky and thick, with a mild, slightly sweet flavour from the eel balanced by the savoury depth of fish sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 112.5 kcal, 6.25 g protein, 3.75 g fat (of which 1.0 g is saturated), and 12.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and salt. Eel contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), making this dish mildly cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory. The rice porridge base has a high glycemic index (~72), so portion control is important for individuals managing blood glucose. A standard serving is one bowl of approximately 400 g, delivering around 450 kcal. The dish is popular in central Vietnam, particularly in Nghệ An province, and is served as a breakfast or light meal. It is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains fish and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese eel porridge as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw freshwater eel and plain rice congee, weighted by typical preparation ratios, and cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA data. The input record values are consistent with this blended estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice congee from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Vietnamese eel porridge as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA table at the composite-dish level as of 2026. Individual eel and rice component data exist but cannot be reliably combined without validated recipe weights. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.03 g per 100 g, consistent with trace levels from cooking oil used in shallot frying; this is a plausible trace value and not confirmed zero. All core macros (protein_g 6.25, fat_g 3.75, carbohydrate_g 12.5) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with the described dish composition. Halal status is marked false as eel (Monopterus albus, swamp eel) is considered impermissible (haram) under Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence, which is predominant in Singapore and Malaysia; consumers should verify with their religious authority. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on rice congee from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Vietnamese eel porridge was found in approved sources.
